content.text: Every individual and household requires goods and services for daily life. Since most people cannot produce everything they need themselves, they rely on others. This creates a network of exchange that leads to prosperity.

content.title: Adam Smith's Insight
content.text: The 18th-century economist Adam Smith observed that 'Prosperity emanates from the market that develops when people need goods and services that they can't create themselves.'

The goods we use reach us through various economic activities: Primary (producing raw materials), Secondary (manufacturing finished goods), and Tertiary (providing services like transport and retail).
A place where people buy and sell goods and services is called a market. In various Indian regions, it is known by names like bazaar or haat (Hindi) and mārukatté (Kannada).

content.title: Needs vs. Wants
content.text: In economics, a 'Need' is something essential for survival (food, water, shelter), whereas a 'Want' is something desired but not essential (mobile phones, luxury items).
Markets can be physical places you visit or online platforms where transactions happen digitally. Beyond goods, markets also connect people, traditions, and ideas.
In 16th-century Karnataka, the Hampi Bazaar was a prosperous centre of trade for the Vijayanagara Empire. It was located opposite the Virupaksha temple and was famous globally for its abundance.

content.title: Goods in Hampi Bazaar
items: Animals like cows, rabbits, horses, and birds
Luxury items like gold jewels, rubies, diamonds, and pearls

content.text: Portuguese travellers like Domingos Paes and Fernao Nuniz described Hampi as one of the best-provided cities in the world due to its flourishing trade.

notes.section_title: Market Features and the Price Mechanism

content.text: Every market transaction requires at least two participants: a buyer and a seller. For a transaction to be completed, both must agree on a Price.

content.title: What is Price?
content.text: Price is the mutually agreeable amount at which a buyer is willing to buy and a seller is willing to sell a particular good or service.
Often, the initial price offered by the seller is higher than what the buyer wants to pay. Through negotiation or bargaining, they arrive at an acceptable middle point.

content.title: How Prices are Determined Over Time
items: If the price is too high, buyers will not buy, leading to unsold stock for the seller.
If the price is too low, the seller may incur a loss and stop offering the product.
The balance between the 'quantity offered' by sellers and the 'quantity required' by buyers determines a price that is 'just right' (high enough for the seller, low enough for the buyer).

content.text: Physical markets are where buyers meet sellers in person. These include local weekly markets (haats), neighbourhood shops, and multi-storey malls.

content.title: The Rise of Online Markets
content.text: Online markets allow transactions through apps or websites on phones or computers. Buyers and sellers don't need to meet in person, and goods are delivered directly to the doorstep.
A market where goods are traded within a nation's geographical boundaries is called a domestic market. When trade occurs across borders, it involves international markets.

rows: Selling goods/services produced in one country to a buyer in another country.
Buying goods/services from other countries and bringing them into one's own country.

content.text: For example, India exports software services and engineering goods, while it imports crude petroleum and vegetable oils like palm oil from Malaysia and Indonesia.

notes.section_title: Participants in the Market: Wholesale and Retail

content.text: Wholesalers buy goods in very large quantities directly from manufacturers or farms. This produce is stored in large warehouses called godowns or specialized cold storage facilities for perishables.

content.title: Mandi
content.text: Agricultural produce bought by wholesalers is often brought to large wholesale markets called mandis for further distribution.
Retailers or shopkeepers buy smaller quantities from wholesalers and sell them to final consumers. Retail stores include grocery shops, salons, and restaurants.

In online markets, manufacturers often send bulk stock to the warehouse of an 'aggregator' (a website or app like Amazon). The aggregator then combines offers from multiple sellers and delivers them to the buyer.

topics.topic_name: Chain of Supply Case Study: Surat Textile Market

content.text: Surat is Asia's oldest textile market. The supply chain moves from raw cotton clusters (in Maharashtra and Gujarat) to power looms for weaving, then to dyeing units, and finally to wholesale markets for global distribution.

content.title: Diamond Industry
content.text: Surat also hosts the world's largest diamond industry, involving 1.5 million artisans in cutting and polishing diamonds on a massive scale.

notes.section_title: The Role of Markets in People's Lives

content.text: Beyond money, markets build trusted long-lasting relationships between families and their local grocers or tailors. They also serve as a melting pot of cultures.

content.title: Ima Keithal (Manipur)
content.text: Located in Imphal, Ima Keithal is a unique market entirely owned and run by about 3,000 women. It is a major source of income and a place where communities exchange ideas and traditions.
Small traditions, like giving a bit of haldi and kumkum (turmeric and vermilion) for free as a sign of goodwill, show that markets have non-economic values too.

notes.section_title: The Role of Government in Markets

content.text: To protect buyers and sellers, the government monitors prices. For example, it sets a Maximum Price for life-saving drugs and a Minimum Price (MSP) for agricultural crops to prevent losses for farmers.

content.title: Minimum Wages
content.text: The government also sets minimum wages to ensure that employees receive fair payments from their employers.
The government intervenes to mitigate negative effects (like pollution from factories) and ensures that manufacturers follow safety standards.

content.title: Kautilya's Vision
content.text: Ancient texts like the Arthashastra show that even historically, rulers made rules to ensure traders didn't exploit buyers, such as giving extra quantity to compensate for loss during measuring.
Government also provides Public Goods like parks, roads, and policing, where producers wouldn't expect to make a profit but are essential for all citizens.
Consumers can assess quality by checking product labels for info like MRP, date of manufacture, expiry date, and batch number. Most importantly, one must look for government certification marks.

content.title: Key Certification Marks in India
items: FSSAI: Marks food safety and standards on food packages.
ISI Mark: Ensures quality for electrical appliances, construction materials, and tyres.
AGMARK: Certification for agricultural products like cereals, pulses, and honey.
BEE STAR Rating: Indicates energy efficiency (Higher stars = less electricity used).

content.text: In addition to labels, modern consumers often use online reviews and feedback from other buyers to decide which products to purchase.

glossary.definition: A place or platform where people buy and sell goods and services.
The act of buying and selling or exchanging goods and services between people or countries.
The amount at which a buyer is willing to buy and a seller is willing to sell a particular product.
Selling goods or services produced in one's own country to a buyer in another country.
Buying goods or services from other countries and bringing them into one's own country.
A participant who buys goods in large quantities from producers and supplies them to retailers.
A shopkeeper who sells goods in smaller quantities meant for final consumption.
An online website or app that organizes and combines offers from multiple sellers and sells them at one place.
Services or commodities like parks and roads that are accessible to all society members and provided by the government.
Food Safety and Standards Authority of India; its symbol indicates that food has been tested and is safe to consume.
A rating given by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency to show how much energy an electronic appliance uses.

summary_points: A market is a network of exchange where buyers and sellers agree on a price for goods and services, fulfilling mutual needs and wants.
The price of a product is determined over time by the interaction between the quantity required by buyers (demand) and the quantity offered by sellers (supply).
Modern markets include physical spaces like weekly haats and malls, as well as digital platforms and online shopping applications.
International trade involves exporting goods to other countries and importing products produced outside the nation's boundaries.
The chain of supply typically moves from manufacturers to wholesalers (bulk storage), to retailers (neighbourhood shops), and finally to consumers.
Markets are not just economic institutions but also social spaces that foster trusted relationships and cultural exchange, like Manipur's Ima Keithal.
The government regulates markets by controlling prices of essentials, setting minimum agricultural prices and wages, and providing public goods.
Quality and safety in markets are ensured through strict government standards and certification marks like FSSAI, ISI, and AGMARK.
Energy-efficient appliances marked with higher BEE STAR ratings benefit both consumers (lower bills) and the environment.
Consumer awareness involves checking product labels for important details and using feedback from other buyers to make informed choices.
